text: The surgeon initiates a laparoscopic cholecystectomy using a Veress needle technique, preparing for the creation of pneumoperitoneum.
The surgeon inserts laparoscopic ports into the abdominal cavity, establishing access points for the instruments used in the cholecystectomy procedure.
After inserting the varus needle and achieving pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon inserts a 5mm trocar in the supraumbilical region.
Two additional 5mm trocars are inserted, one in the epigastric region lateral to the falciform ligament and another in the right lumbar region, facilitating access for laparoscopic instruments.
A twelve millimeter trocar is inserted into the left hypochondrium, complementing the previously placed trocars for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon removes the varus needle under direct visualization, completing the initial setup for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The second assistant uses a grasper to hold the gallbladder fundus, retracting it towards the top right side of the abdomen to facilitate the surgeon's focus on the neck of the gallbladder.
The operator grasps the gallbladder from its neck and retracts it laterally, facilitating exposure of the surgical field for further dissection.
The surgeon begins dissection to separate and cut the adhesions between the duodenum and the gallbladder, using precise surgical techniques to clear the anatomical space between these structures.
The surgeon uses diathermy to cauterize tissue, likely focusing on the area around the gallbladder neck as it is retracted laterally and towards the top right side of the abdomen.
The surgeon uses the back of the hook instrument to perform blunt dissection, manipulating the tissue around the gallbladder neck with lateral retraction provided by the assistant.
The surgeon visually identifies the anatomical structures within Calot's triangle, focusing on the cystic duct and artery as the gallbladder is retracted laterally.
The surgeon visualizes the gallbladder, achieving a clear dissection both laterally and medially, with the gallbladder retracted from its neck.
The surgeon uses suction and irrigation to assist with the dissection, applying these techniques without direct visualization to facilitate tissue separation.
The surgeon visualizes a small branch of the cystic artery, skeletizes it, and applies a clip to secure the vessel.
The surgeon performs further dissection to enhance the visualization of anatomical elements within the Callot triangle, ensuring clear exposure of the structures.
The surgeon applies a LigaClip to the clearly visualized and skeletized cystic artery, securing it with a 10mm clip.
The surgeon uses laparoscopic scissors to cut the cystic artery between the applied clips, completing the division of the artery.
The surgeon uses a death hemi hook to further skeletize the cystic duct, enhancing its visualization by carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses the same technique to ligate and cut the cystic duct, applying clips and cutting with laparoscopic scissors.
The surgeon begins the retrograde cholecystectomy, carefully dissecting the gallbladder from the liver bed, ensuring clear visualization of anatomical structures.
The second assistant grasps the gallbladder from its reflection with the liver, allowing the surgeon to dissect the peritoneum and proceed with completing the cholecystectomy.
The surgeon uses an endopouch retriever to extract the gallbladder specimen through a twelve-millimeter port.
After the specimen is extracted, the surgeon performs accurate hemostasis on the cystic plate, ensuring no bleeding is visible in the surgical field.
We always encourage a good washout with normal saline.
The surgeon extracts the twelve millimeter port and closes the fascia using Netibond 2-0 suture and an EndoClose device.
The remaining trocars are extracted from the abdominal ports, concluding the procedure.
